mn152:1.2: at one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Gajaṅgalā, in Suveḷu Grove.
mn152:10.1: And how, Ānanda, is one a trainee on the path?
mn152:10.10: In this way, Ānanda, one is a trainee on the path.
mn152:10.2: Here, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has seen form [*rūpaṁ*] with the eye, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:10.3: Because of that arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant, he is troubled, ashamed, and disgusted.
mn152:10.4: Having heard a sound with the ear …
mn152:10.5: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn152:10.6: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn152:10.7: having touched a tangible object with the body …
mn152:10.8: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:10.9: Because of that arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant, he is troubled, ashamed, and disgusted.
mn152:11-15.1: And how, Ānanda, is one noble, with developed *indriyas* [*bhāvitindriyo*]?
mn152:11-15.2: Here, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has seen form [*rūpaṁ*] with the eye, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:11-15.3: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a non-repulsive *saññā* toward the repulsive [*paṭikūle appaṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a non-repulsive *saññā* [*appaṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:11-15.4: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a repulsive *saññā* toward the non-repulsive [*appaṭikūle paṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a repulsive *saññā* [*paṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:11-15.5: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a non-repulsive *saññā* toward the repulsive and the non-repulsive [*paṭikūle ca appaṭikūle ca appaṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a non-repulsive *saññā* [*appaṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:11-15.6: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a repulsive *saññā* toward the non-repulsive and the repulsive [*appaṭikūle ca paṭikūle ca paṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a repulsive *saññā* [*paṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:11-15.7: If he wishes: ‘Having avoided both the repulsive and the non-repulsive, may I dwell equanimous, with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*],’ he dwells there equanimous, with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*].
mn152:16.1: Again, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has heard a sound with the ear …
mn152:16.10: If he wishes: ‘Having avoided both the repulsive and the non-repulsive, may I dwell equanimous, with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*],’ he dwells there equanimous, with *sati* and full awareness [*sampajāno*].
mn152:16.11: In this way, Ānanda, one is noble, with developed *indriyas* [*bhāvitindriyo*].
mn152:16.2: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn152:16.3: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn152:16.4: having touched a tangible object with the body …
mn152:16.5: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:16.6: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a non-repulsive *saññā* toward the repulsive [*paṭikūle appaṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a non-repulsive *saññā* [*appaṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:16.7: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a repulsive *saññā* toward the non-repulsive [*appaṭikūle paṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a repulsive *saññā* [*paṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:16.8: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a non-repulsive *saññā* toward the repulsive and the non-repulsive [*paṭikūle ca appaṭikūle ca appaṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a non-repulsive *saññā* [*appaṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:16.9: If he wishes: ‘May I dwell with a repulsive *saññā* toward the non-repulsive and the repulsive [*appaṭikūle ca paṭikūle ca paṭikūlasaññī*],’ he dwells there with a repulsive *saññā* [*paṭikūlasaññī*].
mn152:17.1: Thus, Ānanda, I have taught the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one, I have taught the trainee on the path, and I have taught the noble one with developed *indriyas* [*bhāvitindriyo*].
mn152:18.1: What should be done by a teacher who seeks the welfare of his disciples, who is compassionate, out of compassion—that I have done for you.
mn152:18.2: Here, Ānanda, are roots of trees; here are empty huts. Meditate [*jhāyatha*], Ānanda; do not be negligent, lest you later be remorseful. This is my instruction to you.”
mn152:18.4: Satisfied, Venerable Ānanda delighted in the *Bhagavā*'s statement.
mn152:18.5: The Development of the *Indriyas* [*Indriyabhāvanā*] is finished, the tenth.
mn152:18.6: The Chapter on the Dimension of the Six Senses [*Saḷāyatana*] is finished, the fifth.
mn152:19.3: The Six Sixes, and the Six-Sense Dimension [*Saḷāyatanika*],
mn152:19.5: and also the Development of the *Indriyas* [*Indriyabhāvanā*],
mn152:19.6: the chapter, the fifth on Instruction.
mn152:2.1: Then the brahmin student Uttara, a pupil of Pārāsivi, approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
mn152:2.10: For a blind person, Uttara, does not see form [*rūpaṁ*] with the eye, and a deaf person does not hear sound with the ear.”
mn152:2.11: When this was said, the brahmin student Uttara, the pupil of Pārāsivi, sat silent, embarrassed, shoulders drooping, downcast, brooding, and unable to reply.
mn152:2.2: When the courteous and memorable conversation was finished, he sat to one side. The *Bhagavā* said this to the brahmin student Uttara, the pupil of Pārāsivi, as he was sitting to one side:
mn152:2.3: “Uttara, does the brahmin Pārāsivi teach his disciples the development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanaṁ*]?”
mn152:2.4: “He does, Master Gotama, the brahmin Pārāsivi teaches his disciples the development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanaṁ*].”
mn152:2.5: “But in what way, Uttara, does the brahmin Pārāsivi teach his disciples the development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanaṁ*]?”
mn152:2.6: “Here, Master Gotama, one does not see form [*rūpaṁ*] with the eye, one does not hear sound with the ear—
mn152:2.7: in this way, Master Gotama, the brahmin Pārāsivi teaches his disciples the development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanaṁ*].”
mn152:2.8: “In that case, Uttara, a blind person will have developed *indriyas* [*bhāvitindriyo*], a deaf person will have developed *indriyas* [*bhāvitindriyo*],
mn152:2.9: according to the word of the brahmin Pārāsivi.
mn152:20.0: This is the summary of the chapters:
mn152:20.1: At Devadaha and One by One,
mn152:20.3: and the Six-Sense Dimension [*Saḷāyatana*] are the chapters
mn152:20.6: Adorned with three groups of fifty, the whole
mn152:3.1: Then, knowing that the brahmin student Uttara, the pupil of Pārāsivi, was silent, embarrassed, shoulders drooping, downcast, brooding, and unable to reply, the *Bhagavā* addressed Venerable Ānanda:
mn152:3.2: “Ānanda, the brahmin Pārāsivi teaches his disciples the development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanaṁ*] in one way, but the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one is otherwise.”
mn152:3.3: “It is time for this, *Bhagavā*; it is time for this, Fortunate One,
mn152:3.4: that the *Bhagavā* teach the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanaṁ*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one. Having heard it from the *Bhagavā*, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn152:3.5: “Then listen, Ānanda, attend carefully; I will speak.”
mn152:4.1: “And how, Ānanda, is there the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one?
mn152:4.10: Just as, Ānanda, a person with sight might open their eyes after closing them, or close their eyes after opening them;
mn152:4.11: so too, Ānanda, for anyone whose arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases this swiftly, this quickly, this easily, and equanimity [*upekkhā*] becomes established—
mn152:4.12: this is called, Ānanda, the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one with regard to forms [*rūpesu*] known by the eye.
mn152:4.2: Here, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has seen form [*rūpaṁ*] with the eye, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:4.4: ‘This pleasant has arisen in me, the unpleasant has arisen, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant has arisen.
mn152:4.6: This is peaceful, this is refined, namely—
mn152:4.8: That arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases for him;
mn152:5.1: Again, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has heard a sound with the ear, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:5.10: so too, Ānanda, for anyone whose arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases this swiftly, this quickly, this easily, and equanimity [*upekkhā*] becomes established—
mn152:5.11: this is called, Ānanda, the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one with regard to sounds known by the ear.
mn152:5.3: ‘This pleasant has arisen in me, the unpleasant has arisen, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant has arisen.
mn152:5.5: This is peaceful, this is refined, namely—
mn152:5.7: That arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases for him;
mn152:5.9: Just as, Ānanda, a strong person might snap their fingers with very little difficulty;
mn152:6.1: Again, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has smelled an odor with the nose, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:6.10: so too, Ānanda, for anyone whose arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases this swiftly, this quickly, this easily, and equanimity [*upekkhā*] becomes established—
mn152:6.11: this is called, Ānanda, the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one with regard to odors known by the nose.
mn152:6.3: ‘This pleasant has arisen in me, the unpleasant has arisen, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant has arisen.
mn152:6.5: This is peaceful, this is refined, namely—
mn152:6.7: That arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases for him;
mn152:6.9: Just as, Ānanda, drops of water roll off a lotus leaf that is slightly tilted and do not remain;
mn152:7.1: Again, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has tasted a flavor with the tongue, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:7.10: so too, Ānanda, for anyone whose arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases this swiftly, this quickly, this easily, and equanimity [*upekkhā*] becomes established—
mn152:7.11: this is called, Ānanda, the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one with regard to flavors known by the tongue.
mn152:7.3: ‘This pleasant has arisen in me, the unpleasant has arisen, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant has arisen.
mn152:7.5: This is peaceful, this is refined, namely—
mn152:7.7: That arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases for him;
mn152:7.9: Just as, Ānanda, a strong person might gather a ball of saliva on the tip of the tongue and spit it out with little difficulty;
mn152:8.1: Again, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has touched a tangible object with the body, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:8.10: so too, Ānanda, for anyone whose arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases this swiftly, this quickly, this easily, and equanimity [*upekkhā*] becomes established—
mn152:8.11: this is called, Ānanda, the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one with regard to tangible objects known by the body.
mn152:8.3: ‘This pleasant has arisen in me, the unpleasant has arisen, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant has arisen.
mn152:8.5: This is peaceful, this is refined, namely—
mn152:8.7: That arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases for him;
mn152:8.9: Just as, Ānanda, a strong person might extend a bent arm, or bend an extended arm;
mn152:9.1: Again, Ānanda, when a *bhikkhu* has cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, the pleasant arises, the unpleasant arises, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant arises.
mn152:9.10: Slow, Ānanda, is the falling of the drops of water, yet they would quickly come to destruction and exhaustion;
mn152:9.11: so too, Ānanda, for anyone whose arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases this swiftly, this quickly, this easily, and equanimity [*upekkhā*] becomes established—
mn152:9.12: this is called, Ānanda, the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one with regard to *dhammas* known by the mind.
mn152:9.13: In this way, Ānanda, there is the unsurpassed development of the *indriyas* [*indriyabhāvanā*] in the *vinaya* of the noble one.
mn152:9.3: ‘This pleasant has arisen in me, the unpleasant has arisen, and the pleasant-and-unpleasant has arisen.
mn152:9.5: This is peaceful, this is refined, namely—
mn152:9.7: That arisen pleasant, arisen unpleasant, and arisen pleasant-and-unpleasant ceases for him;
mn152:9.9: Just as, Ānanda, a strong person might drop two or three drops of water onto an iron cauldron heated all day.
